segments.text: Well, good afternoon.
Welcome to the Committee Selection Committee.
Um, so thank you for talking to us this afternoon.
What I'd ask is if you could introduce yourselves, uh,
to the committee and those watching online,
and, uh, then ask you to, uh, you know,
make an opening, um, statement.
And then from there we'll move to questions from Emmas.
Great. Thank you. Lieutenant Coto car.
Thank you Chair, and good afternoon everyone.
Uh, so I'm Jason Delamore.
I'm the Chief executive of, uh, the Laies Commission,
which I'll call Lotto New Zealand.
Uh, and with me, I've got chair by board Mark Todd next
to me, Jeff Haywood, our Chief Financial Officer,
and Will Hein, our head of corporate comms.
So, good afternoon to you all.
Uh, so thanks for the opportunity to appear before you.
As part of Lottos annual Review, we take our accountability
to New Zealand as a
and Parliament Extreme extremely seriously.
Although Lotto does not receive taxpayer money,
we are a Crown entity with a statutory monopoly.
And the revenue we generate
through Lawrie Games is ultimately public money
that supports literally thousands
of community organizations.
We see this, this review as an opportunity to demonstrate
how responsibly and transparently we
have delivered on our mandate.
So Lotto is a bit of history.
Lotto was established back in 1987 under the Gambling Act,
and we exist to provide safe games that allow New Zealanders
to play and win while contributing money back
to Kiwi communities.
Through the Lotto Grants board.
In the past 38 years,
we have returned more than $6.7 billion back to communities.
The Lotto Grants board is an independent government entity,
and, and Lotto has no input into where
that funding should be allocated.
We aim to provide these strong,
sustainable community returns while continually
strengthening the safety of our products to reduce risks
around problem gambling and underage gambling.
Everything we do is shaped by balancing these two things.
The last time Lotto appeared in person
before the committee was two years ago.
Since then, we have reported two record financial results.
We returned $434 million to the lot of grant board in FY 24
and $395 million in the past financial year,
benefiting thousands of organizations.
I'm proud that we've done so well, keeping reports
of gambling harm stable under our target
of one person per 10,000 customers.
In fact, on a per capita basis, gambling harm reports
to the Ministry of Health related
to Lotto have actually halved since 2017.
Under our strategy to 2029, we are aiming
to return $412 million in annual profits
to the lottery grants board.
To do that, we've set a pathway for us
to boldly transform our customer experience.
A major step forward in this regard happened in September
with a successful delivery of our Edge project.
This involved replacing our core and online gaming system
and updating the physical hardware in more than 1100
stores across the country.
Uh, this project was completed on time
and within Budget Edge,
and the projects that will follow are very important.
It's crucial that we remain able
to provide a modern lottery products safely
and responsibly, given the rapidly changing
competitive environment that we're in.
And this includes the forthcoming licensing
of online casinos within New Zealand.
Part of our statutory role is
to advise the government on lotteries,
and we've actively, actively engaged in this capacity in the
past 12 months, noting the direction of the government
around fiscal discipline.
We are proud to have strict cost controls in place
and close the year, 10.2% below our budget.
One of the things I'm most pleased about is our strong
and engaged workforce, especially being able to say that
that's having just delivered a project
that requires significant
and sustained effort from the whole team
across the organization.
Lastly, we're also very proud
to be ranked number five on Kattar's Corporate Reputation
Index in 2025, and that reflects our strong brand trust
and connection to New Zealanders,
and that improved from 12th place two years ago.
So you have our responses to the annual review, uh,
questions as well as our annual report
and corporate planning documents, and we're welcome.
Any questions you may have.
Thank you very much. Yeah. First Scott, thank you.
Um, I appreciate you support Kiwis to play and win,
but possibly use encourage Kiwis to play
and lose more regularly.
Would that be accurate?
Uh, in terms of the odds? Yes. Yes. Thank you.

it's all about lots of Kiwis just contributing a little bit.

around $20 to put it into context.

source_metadata.title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 4)
source_metadata.description:
Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee

2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Lotteries Commission

Hearing of evidence:

New Zealand Lotteries Commission

Mark Todd, Board Chair
Jason Delamore, Chief Executive
Jeff Hayward, Chief Financial Officer
Will Hine, Head of Corporate Communications
